summary: Two-story rectangular building with a stepped curved wooden roof, stone/brick mid-wall banding, and continuous horizontal window rows on both floors. Surrounded by a low white border ledge and set into a leveled dirt plot. Visible from multiple angles; roof is symmetrical along the long axis.

shape.overall_form: rectangular prism base with a stepped barrel-style wooden roof centered on top
shape.symmetry: bilateral along the long (roof ridge) axis

materials.name: slab_stone
materials.role: roof edging and possibly slab accents

elements: continuous horizontal window strip on each floor (multiple 1x1 glass panes separated by frames)
raised stepped wooden roof with center ridge and symmetrical steps
two distinct horizontal wall bands (light top band and darker stone band below)
perimeter low white ledge/bench around base
rebuild_hints: Create a rectangular two-floor shell; each floor height appears to be 3 blocks tall (ground floor, middle band, second floor).
Place a continuous row of windows centered on each long wall spanning most of the length, framed by stonebrick band below and a light block band above.
Use cobblestone (or similar textured block) at vertical corners extending full building height.
Add a stepped curved roof made from wood planks: slope up in 1-block steps from both long sides to a central ridge; roof extends slightly beyond walls with stone slab edging near the ridge.
Surround the base with a single-block-high white ledge (quartz) forming a rectangular rim set one block out from the walls on the long sides.
uncertainties: Exact block types: light horizontal band may be quartz (assumed) but could be another light block (concrete, wool).
Precise footprint dimensions: top-down images show rounded roof edges that slightly reduce visible roof width; width/depth integers are estimates.
Ground border material and thickness are inferred as quartz slabs/blocks; could be smooth stone variants.
Whether interior floors/partitions exist is not visible; internal layout unknown.
Roof ridge detail includes small stone slab features; exact slab vs full block usage uncertain.
